class_schedule.topic: Why Teach Literature? Justifications and Controversies
learning_objectives: Articulate multiple justifications for teaching literature: aesthetic, ethical, cognitive, and cultural
Analyze the controversies surrounding canon selection and required reading lists
Understand the difference between teaching about literature and teaching through literature

lecture_notes.introduction: Before we can teach literature well, we must be able to answer the question: why teach literature at all? This course prepares you to teach literature at the secondary or post-secondary level by grounding pedagogical practice in both literary knowledge and educational theory.
key_concepts: Nussbaum's argument: literature cultivates empathy, critical thinking, and democratic citizenship
The canon wars: who decides what students read and why
Aesthetic justification: literature as an end in itself vs. literature as a means to other ends
Student-centered pedagogy: starting with what students bring to the text, not what they lack
The coverage model vs. the depth model: surveying many texts vs. reading fewer texts closely
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a reading list for a 10th-grade English class, then defending the selections to a skeptical school board — a role-play that forces students to articulate their educational values
setup_requirements: Sample syllabi from high school and college courses

review.value: Nussbaum argues that reading fiction develops the narrative imagination — the ability to see the world from another person's perspective — which is essential for democratic citizenship and ethical life
The impulse to 'cover' a period or tradition often means racing through texts so quickly that students cannot develop the close reading skills that are the actual goal of literary education

class_schedule.topic: Designing a Literature Course: Learning Objectives, Text Selection, and Assessment
learning_objectives: Write measurable learning objectives for a literature course using Bloom's taxonomy
Design assessments that align with learning objectives and measure literary skills, not just content recall
Select texts that balance canonical importance, student engagement, and diverse representation
readings: Wiggins and McTighe, Understanding by Design, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Good course design starts with the end: what should students be able to do after taking this course that they could not do before? Every text, assignment, and activity must serve those outcomes.
key_concepts: Backward design: start with desired outcomes, then design assessments, then plan instruction
Bloom's taxonomy applied to literature: remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create
Text selection criteria: literary quality, accessibility, diversity, and teachability
Assessment design: moving beyond quizzes and essays to authentic assessments of literary thinking
Scaffolding: sequencing texts and activities so skills build progressively
lecture_notes.demonstration: Backward design workshop: students design a four-week unit on a theme of their choice, starting with the final assessment and working backward to daily lessons

review.value: Wiggins and McTighe's framework: identify desired results (what students should understand and be able to do), determine acceptable evidence (how you will know they can do it), then plan learning experiences (how you will teach it) — in that order

Assessments that ask students to do what literary scholars actually do — analyze a text, construct an argument, make an interpretation — rather than recall facts about literary history or identify terms on a matching quiz

class_schedule.topic: Leading Discussion: Socratic Seminars and Close Reading in the Classroom
learning_objectives: Facilitate a text-based discussion that develops student thinking rather than testing recall
Design questions at multiple levels of Bloom's taxonomy to scaffold discussion
Manage discussion dynamics: drawing out quiet students, redirecting dominant voices, and handling controversial interpretations
readings: Selected articles on discussion-based pedagogy (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Discussion is not a teacher asking questions and students guessing what the teacher wants to hear. Genuine discussion requires students to think together about a text, building interpretations collaboratively through evidence and argument.
key_concepts: The Socratic seminar: student-led discussion grounded in the text, with the teacher as facilitator
Question design: recall questions, analytical questions, evaluative questions, and connective questions
Wait time: the power of silence — allowing students time to think before expecting answers
Inclusive discussion: strategies for ensuring all voices are heard, not just the loudest
Handling controversy: managing disagreement productively without shutting down difficult conversations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Practice Socratic seminar: half the class facilitates a discussion of a short text while the other half observes and provides feedback on facilitation techniques

review.value: A student-led discussion in which participants engage directly with a text, asking questions of each other rather than answering the teacher's questions — the teacher facilitates but does not direct, creating space for genuine inquiry

Research shows that teachers typically wait less than one second after asking a question before calling on a student. Extending wait time to 3-5 seconds dramatically increases the quality and length of student responses and the number of students who participate

class_schedule.topic: Teaching Writing About Literature: From Response to Analysis
learning_objectives: Design writing assignments that scaffold students from personal response to analytical argument
Provide effective feedback on student literary analysis
Understand the relationship between reading skill and writing skill in literary education
readings: Sample student essays at different skill levels (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Teaching students to write about literature is teaching them to think about literature. The writing assignments we design determine what kind of thinking students practice — and effective assignments scaffold students from where they are to where we want them to be.
key_concepts: The response-to-analysis continuum: moving students from personal reaction to evidence-based argument
Assignment scaffolding: journal, response, close reading, analysis, research essay
Rubric design: making expectations transparent and assessment consistent
Feedback that teaches: commenting on patterns rather than individual errors, asking questions rather than making corrections
The thesis problem: why students struggle with arguable claims and how to teach thesis construction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calibration exercise: students read and assess a student literary analysis essay using a provided rubric, then compare their assessments and discuss discrepancies

review.value: Moving students from low-stakes response (journal entries, informal reactions) through guided close reading exercises to formal analytical essays — each step builds the skills needed for the next

Students default to observational claims ('Shakespeare uses metaphor') because arguable claims require taking an interpretive risk. Teaching thesis construction means teaching students to make claims that a reasonable reader could disagree with — and then to defend them with evidence.

class_schedule.topic: Diversity, Inclusion, and the Literature Classroom
learning_objectives: Design an inclusive literature curriculum that represents diverse voices without tokenism
Address sensitive content — race, sexuality, trauma, violence — in the classroom with care and intentionality
Understand the concept of 'windows and mirrors': texts as reflections of students' own experience and views into others'
readings: Bishop, 'Mirrors, Windows, and Sliding Glass Doors' (essay)
Sample diverse syllabi from award-winning teachers (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Every reading list is a statement about whose stories matter. An inclusive literature curriculum ensures that all students see themselves reflected in the texts they read and encounter perspectives different from their own — not as a political project but as an educational imperative.
key_concepts: Windows and mirrors: students need texts that reflect their own experience (mirrors) and texts that show them worlds they have not experienced (windows)
Beyond tokenism: including diverse texts as integral to the curriculum, not as add-ons during designated months
Teaching sensitive content: content warnings, discussion norms, and the balance between safety and challenge
Culturally responsive pedagogy: connecting texts to students' cultural knowledge and lived experience
Whose classics? Expanding the canon without abandoning the commitment to literary quality
lecture_notes.demonstration: Redesigning a traditional British Literature survey to include diverse voices without losing canonical texts, then discussing the trade-offs and principles behind the decisions

review.value: Bishop's metaphor: literature should provide mirrors that reflect students' own identities and experiences, windows that show them the experiences of others, and sliding glass doors that invite them to walk into unfamiliar worlds

Teaching that connects academic content to students' cultural backgrounds, experiences, and ways of knowing — not to make content 'easier' but to make it more meaningful and to honor the knowledge students bring to the classroom

questions.question: What is backward design, and how does it differ from the traditional approach of planning a course by selecting texts first?
questions.answer: Backward design starts with identifying desired outcomes (what students should know and be able to do), then designs assessments (how you will measure whether they can do it), and only then selects texts and plans instruction. Traditional planning starts with texts (what will we read?) and designs activities and assessments afterward. Backward design ensures that every text and assignment serves the learning goals; traditional planning risks choosing texts out of habit or personal preference without clear alignment to outcomes.

rubric.key: Explains the advantage of backward design

questions.question: What is the difference between 'teaching about literature' and 'teaching through literature'? Give an example of each.
questions.answer: Teaching about literature treats literary content as the goal: students learn literary history, terminology, and the 'meanings' of canonical texts. Teaching through literature uses texts as vehicles for developing transferable skills: close reading, analytical writing, empathy, critical thinking, and argumentation. Example of 'about': memorizing the dates of the Romantic period and the names of Romantic poets. Example of 'through': using a Romantic poem to practice close reading skills that transfer to any text the student encounters.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between the two approaches
Identifies transferable skills as the goal of 'teaching through'

questions.question: What is 'wait time' in classroom discussion, and why does extending it improve discussion quality?
questions.answer: Wait time is the pause between when a teacher asks a question and when they call on a student (or rephrase/answer the question themselves). Research shows teachers typically wait less than one second. Extending wait time to 3-5 seconds improves discussion because students need time to formulate thoughtful responses; short wait time rewards students who speak before thinking and penalizes reflective thinkers, reducing both the quality and the diversity of participation.

rubric.key: Cites the research finding about typical wait time
Explains why extending it improves quality and participation

questions.question: Why do students struggle with writing arguable thesis statements in literary analysis? What is one strategy for teaching thesis construction?
questions.answer: Students default to observational or factual claims ('Shakespeare uses metaphor in Hamlet') because arguable claims require taking an interpretive risk — asserting something a reasonable reader might disagree with. One effective strategy: give students two competing interpretations of a passage and ask them to choose one and defend it. This forces them to take a position and to recognize that interpretation involves argument, not just description.

rubric.key: Explains why observations are easier than arguments

major_assignments.title: Course Design Project
major_assignments.description: Design a complete four-week literature unit for a specified grade level (your choice of secondary or post-secondary). The unit must include learning objectives, a rationale for text selection, daily lesson plans, assessments aligned to objectives, and a diversity and inclusion statement.
objectives: Apply backward design principles to a literature unit
Select texts with clear rationale addressing literary quality, accessibility, and diversity
Design assessments that authentically measure literary skills
Create lesson plans that scaffold skills progressively
deliverables: Unit plan with learning objectives, text list with rationales, and daily lesson plans for four weeks
Two complete assessments (one formative, one summative) with rubrics
Diversity and inclusion statement explaining text selection principles
Teach one 15-minute lesson from the unit to the class and receive peer feedback

criteria: Learning objectives are measurable and appropriate for the grade level
Backward design is evident: objectives, assessments, and instruction align
Lesson plans are detailed enough for another teacher to follow
Scaffolding builds skills progressively across the four weeks
Texts are appropriate for the grade level and serve the learning objectives
Selection demonstrates commitment to diversity without tokenism
Rationales articulate specific reasons for each text's inclusion
Text sequence creates productive intellectual progression
Assessments measure the stated learning objectives
Rubrics are clear and would produce consistent scoring across teachers
Both formative and summative assessment are included
Assessments ask students to do literary thinking, not just recall
Mini-lesson is well-prepared and engaging
Teaching demonstrates command of both content and pedagogy
Student interaction is facilitated, not dominated
Peer feedback is received gracefully and reflected upon
Well-designed unit demonstrating backward design principles
Thoughtful text selection balancing quality, diversity, and teachability
Authentic assessments measuring genuine literary skills
Confident, student-centered teaching demonstration

course_design_project.description: Complete four-week literature unit design with teaching demonstration

teaching_reflections.description: Weekly reflections on pedagogical readings and classroom observations

quizzes.description: Quizzes on pedagogical concepts and frameworks

peer_teaching.description: Quality of teaching demonstrations and peer feedback

final_portfolio.description: Teaching philosophy statement and revised unit plan

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory, especially on teaching demonstration days. More than 2 unexcused absences result in a letter grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Unit plan components penalized 10% per day late.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer feedback is collaborative. All unit designs are individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All lesson plans and assessments must be original. Adapting published materials is acceptable with citation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Word processing software, access to curriculum standards documents, and LMS access.
